nativeRange.habitat: Tropical reef flats, lagoons and shallow rocky substrates, grazing biofilm and algae on hard surfaces; juveniles and adults occur from the low intertidal down to roughly 20 m.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with established algae film
stable rock structures (large snails can bulldoze loose rock)

diet.notes: Voracious grazer on film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and soft green algae across glass, live rock and substrate. Generally considered effective against film and diatom outbreaks but does not reliably clear long hair algae or macroalgae. When tank algae is depleted, supplement with dried nori/seaweed sheets or sinking herbivore wafers; adequate calcium (~400-450 ppm) and magnesium (~1250-1350 ppm) are needed to maintain shell integrity.

goodWith: other cleanup crew snails (Astraea, Nassarius, Ceriths)
avoidWith: predatory wrasses (Coris, Thalassoma) that crush snails

summary: A pyramid-shelled reef-safe algae grazer and cleanup-crew workhorse. Generally considered one of the most reliable saltwater snails because it can right itself when toppled, unlike Astraea snails.
careNotes: Trochus Snail is an umbrella name for several closely related Trochidae species sold under the genus name Trochus in the reef hobby — most commonly Trochus radiatus and Trochus histrio, plus Tectus niloticus (still widely marketed as 'Trochus niloticus' or 'Banded Trochus' despite being reclassified out of Trochus to Tectus). All share essentially identical reef-aquarium care: pyramid-shaped shells, herbivorous grazing of film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria and soft green algae, and a strong preference for stable reef chemistry (pH ~8.1-8.4, dKH ~8-12, SG ~1.023-1.025, temperature ~23-26 C). A major husbandry advantage over the similar-looking Astraea Snail (Astraea tecta) is that Trochus can right themselves when knocked over — Astraea cannot, and starve upside down unless manually flipped, making Trochus the more forgiving cleanup-crew choice. They are intolerant of copper-based medications like other marine invertebrates. Captive-bred lines are available from ORA and other aquaculture facilities, which generally arrive in better condition than wild-collected stock. Stocking density is commonly cited as one snail per 8-12 L (2-3 US gal) of display volume; over-stocking risks starvation as they quickly graze out their food supply. Adults can bulldoze loose rockwork, so make sure aquascaping is well secured. Source temperature ranges spanned 22-27 C (LiveAquaria 22-26, AquariumBreeder 23-26, Bulk Reef Supply 23-27); averaged toward 23-26 C as the consensus reef-safe band.
breedingNotes: Broadcast spawners — release eggs and sperm into the water column. Larvae have an unusually short planktonic phase of roughly 3-7 days before settling, which makes Trochus relatively practical for commercial aquaculture compared to most marine gastropods. Captive-bred juveniles are available from ORA and similar producers. Hobbyist-scale breeding in display tanks is rare but spontaneous settlement has been reported in mature reef systems.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned no usable species page (general wiki landing only); cross-verification was instead performed against Bulk Reef Supply and AquariumBreeder per the secondary-fallback rule.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder - Trochus Snails Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (T. radiatus 1.7-4 cm, T. histrio 2.5-5 cm), temperature (23-26 C), pH (8.1-8.4), SG (1.021-1.025), kH (8-12), lifespan (2-3 yr), short 3-7 day larval phase, Indo-Pacific to ~20 m.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply - How To Care For Trochus Snails
additional.notes: Third source for temperature (23-27 C), pH (7.7-8.5), SG (1.023-1.026); confirmed self-righting ability vs Astraea and reef-safe cleanup-crew status.

additional.name: Wikipedia - Trochus (genus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy: family Trochidae, order Trochida, class Gastropoda. Confirmed T. radiatus Gmelin 1791 as a valid species; flagged that 'Trochus niloticus' has been reclassified to Tectus niloticus (still sold under the legacy name in the trade).
